---
# ── FILL IN YOUR DETAILS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
title: "Your Post Title"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2025-03-01"                          # YYYY-MM-DD format
description: "One sentence. Make it good."  # Appears in the post listing
categories: [social psychology]             # Add relevant topic tags
image: "images/cover.jpg"                   # Optional cover image
stream: homework
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Citation setup — add a references.bib file to this folder and uncomment:
# bibliography: references.bib
# csl: apa.csl   # optional — APA style is default

toc: true
---

<!-- DELETE THIS BLOCK before submitting ──────────────────────────────────────
STREAM: HOMEWORK

This template is for a well-structured academic post. Aim for 800–1200 words
of main content. Use proper citations. Write clearly enough for a thoughtful
non-psychologist to follow.

Suggested structure:
  1. Opening hook (1–2 paragraphs)
  2. Background / what the research says (2–3 paragraphs)
  3. A key study or finding in detail (1–2 paragraphs)
  4. Critical evaluation (1 paragraph)
  5. So what / takeaway (1 paragraph)
  6. References

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── -->


## Your opening section heading

Start with something that pulls the reader in. A surprising finding, a counter-intuitive claim, a question worth asking. Don't start with "In this post I will...".

Write naturally. You know this topic — write as if you're explaining it to a curious friend who hasn't studied psychology.


## Background

::: {.callout-note}
## Key concept
Define your central concept here clearly, in plain language.
:::

Continue with the background. What does the field say about this?
Cite your sources inline like this [@authorYear].

Multiple citations work like this [@firstAuthor2020; @secondAuthor2021].


## A study in focus

Go deeper on one specific study or line of research. What was the design? What did they find? Why does it matter?


::: {.callout-tip}
## My take
Use this box to clearly separate your opinion from the evidence.
This is good academic practice: claim ownership of your interpretations.
:::


## Critical evaluation

No study is perfect. What are the methodological limitations? What would you want to know that this research doesn't tell you?

::: {.callout-warning}
## Limitation to flag
Use this box to highlight a specific caveat — sampling, measurement, replication, etc.
:::


## Takeaway

What should the reader walk away thinking? One clear paragraph. Make it land.


## References

::: {#refs}
:::

<!-- Paste your BibTeX references into a file called references.bib
     in this folder. Example entry:

@article{loftus1974,
  author  = {Loftus, Elizabeth F. and Palmer, John C.},
  year    = {1974},
  title   = {Reconstruction of automobile destruction},
  journal = {Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior},
  volume  = {13},
  number  = {5},
  pages   = {585--589},
  doi     = {10.1016/S0022-5371(74)80011-3}
}
-->

---

::: {.author-card}

<div>
<div class="author-card-name">Your Name</div>
<div class="author-card-bio">
  Psychology student at Goldsmiths, University of London.
  Write one sentence about your interests here.
</div>
</div>
:::

*This post is published under [CC BY 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).*
